coredata.dc:title: Isolated mung bean protein-pectin nanocomposite film containing true cardamom extract microencapsulation /CeO2 nanoparticles/graphite carbon quantum dots: Investigating fluorescence, photocatalytic and antimicrobial properties
coredata.dc:creator: Sani, Iraj Karimi
Alizadeh, Mohammad
coredata.dc:description: In this study, isolated mung bean protein (IMBP) – apple pectin (AP) nanocomposite film containing of True cardamom extract microencapsulation (TCEM) (0, 3.75 and 7.5 mL/g basic polymer), CeO2 nanoparticle (0, 2.5 and 5 mg/g basic polymer) and CQD (0, 2.5 and 5 mL /g basic polymer) were prepared. Also, structural properies of the nanocomposite film was also investigated after determining the optimized samples. The results showed that with the addition of TCEM/CeO2/CQD the antioxidant capacity, moisture, thickness, solubility, water vapor permeation of the samples increased (p < 0.05). Additionally, with the addition of the microcapsule, the nanoparticle and CQD the tensile strength and elongation length of the film samples increased which was statistically significant (p < 0.05). The results of Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) revealed an electrostatic interaction within the nanocomposite film. The obtained images from X-ray diffraction (XRD) showed reduced crystalline structure in the films containing TCEM. The results of Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) for the samples of the optimized film and the films containing nanoparticles showed better thermal stability compared to the blank. Moreover, the scanning electron microscope (SEM) results showed porosity generation with the addition of the nanoparticles and TCEM nd CQD. Also, the results of antimicrobial activity against E. coli and S. Aureus showed an increase in the diameter of growth inhibition zone following the addition of TCEM/CeO2/CQD and the films displayed high antimicrobial activity. The results of photocatalytic analysis in the samples containing nanoparticles and the optimized sample showed the highest percentage of disintegration.

head.ce:title: Isolated mung bean protein-pectin nanocomposite film containing true cardamom extract microencapsulation /CeO2 nanoparticles/graphite carbon quantum dots: Investigating fluorescence, photocatalytic and antimicrobial properties

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food Science and Technology, Faculty of Agriculture, Urmia University, P.O. Box 57561–51818, Urmia, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Food Science and Technology, Faculty of Agriculture, Urmia University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food Science and Technology, Faculty of Agriculture, Urmia University, P.O. Box 57561–51818, Urmia, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, isolated mung bean protein (IMBP) – apple pectin (AP) nanocomposite film containing of True cardamom extract microencapsulation (TCEM) (0, 3.75 and 7.5 mL/g basic polymer), CeO2 nanoparticle (0, 2.5 and 5 mg/g basic polymer) and CQD (0, 2.5 and 5 mL /g basic polymer) were prepared. Also, structural properies of the nanocomposite film was also investigated after determining the optimized samples. The results showed that with the addition of TCEM/CeO2/CQD the antioxidant capacity, moisture, thickness, solubility, water vapor permeation of the samples increased (p < 0.05). Additionally, with the addition of the microcapsule, the nanoparticle and CQD the tensile strength and elongation length of the film samples increased which was statistically significant (p < 0.05). The results of Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) revealed an electrostatic interaction within the nanocomposite film. The obtained images from X-ray diffraction (XRD) showed reduced crystalline structure in the films containing TCEM. The results of Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) for the samples of the optimized film and the films containing nanoparticles showed better thermal stability compared to the blank. Moreover, the scanning electron microscope (SEM) results showed porosity generation with the addition of the nanoparticles and TCEM nd CQD. Also, the results of antimicrobial activity against E. coli and S. Aureus showed an increase in the diameter of growth inhibition zone following the addition of TCEM/CeO2/CQD and the films displayed high antimicrobial activity. The results of photocatalytic analysis in the samples containing nanoparticles and the optimized sample showed the highest percentage of disintegration.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the last decades, there has been a substantial increase in the use of non-biodegradable plastic materials around the world owing to their favorable strong mechanical and physical applications in packaging (Din et al., 2020; Pirsa et al., 2022). With regard to the environmental issues ensued by the application of plastic materials, biodegradable plastic materials are nowadays favored as they degrade into CO2 and H2O molecules after use (Ahmadi, Ahmadi et al., 2021; Burgos et al., 2017). Meanwhile, biodegradable edible films based on natural biopolymers have gained much attention owing to their desirable properties such as recyclability, biodegradability, cost effectiveness and availability (Pilevar et al., 2019) as well as their potential to reduce food spoilage, preventing oxidation, possibility of incorporating anti-oxidant and antimicrobial compounds, preventing enzymic and non-enzymic browning during storage (Sahraee et al., 2019).

Mung bean (Vigna radiate L.) is an important traditional cereal cultivated and consumed in most Asian countries (Hou et al., 2020). It contains low amount of fat (1–2%) and relatively high amount of protein (19.5 — 33.1%). The main proteins found in the mung bean are globulins (62%), albumins (16.3%), glutelins (13.3%) and prolamins (0.9%) (Kusumah et al., 2020). Additionally, the edible films based on mung bean protein isolate (MPI) can increase the mechanical strength of the films but are not as effective against the permeation of water, oxygen and CO2. The main inviting factors for using such films are availability, low cost, potential for manufacturing films, high levels of available proteins and polyphenols (Zhou et al., 2017; Li et al., 2020).

Pectin, as one of the most complex structural polysaccharides, is can be extracted from the cell walls of plants and has desirable properties such as biocompatibility, flexibility, non-toxicity, high molecular weight and being a natural anion (Nasrollahzadeh et al., 2021). This compound is mainly comprised of D-Galacturonic acid molecules that are bonded via α-(1−4) glycosidic bonds (Liang & Luo, 2020). In addition, pectin-based edible film has inhibiting potential against oxygen and a variety of gases and oils. Also, the water vapor permeation (WVP) of pectin is high but it has weak mechanical performance. This compound can also act as a carrier for antimicrobial and antioxidant agents (Younis & Zhao, 2019).

True cardamom (Elettaria cardamomum), is an herbaceous, perennial, aromatic, ever green plants belonging to the ginger family (Ashokkumar et al., 2020). It is known as a major source for flavonoids, alkaloids, terpenoids, anthocyanins and phenolic compounds (Hamza & Hussein, 2019). True cardamom (TC) has pharmaceutical properties such as anti-oxidant, anti-inflammatory, anti-carcinogenic and anti-microbial features (Souissi et al., 2020). Compounds such as 1–8 cineol, vitamin E, sterols, starch, fiber, iron, camphor, etc. can be found in TC extract (TCE) as well as high amounts of phenolic comompounds (Khorshidi et al., 2021). To preserve its sensitive and desirable compounds and countering the high volatility of the extract, TCE is encapsulated via different methods that can lead to the preservation and manufacturing of new materials with novel features (Tang et al., 2019).

Nowadays the application of metaloxide nanoparticles for improving the package has received much attention owing to their properties such as environmental friendliness, stability against light, chemical stability, high selectivity, low price, low toxicity and UV resistance (De Silva et al., 2017؛ Basavegowda et al., 2020; Azizi-Lalabadi et al., 2019). The nanoparticles, with their innate antimicrobial activity, can lead to food safety against bacteria, fungi and harmful food spoiling viruses and increase the product longevity (Gaikwad et al., 2018; Nikolic et al., 2021; Sani et al., 2021). Meanwhile, the application of cerium oxide (CeO2) in polymeric films can increase the physical properties of the film layers and generate antibacterial traits in the films. These nanoparticles have high refractive and transition indices in the visible light spectrum, are resistant to UV light and can store high amounts of oxygen (Lu et al., 2020). Additionally, the carbon quantum particles are a group of quantum dots with distinct characteristics such as high stability, remarkable optical attributes, good water solibility, low toxicity, high surface to volume ratio and antibacterial and antioxidant features. These characteristics have led to their widespread application in the fields of bioimaging, biosensors, UV barrier and packaging (Kousheh et al., 2020). The purpose of this work is to investigate the effects of graphite carbon quantum dots containing different concentrations of TCE micro-encapsulation and CeO2 nanoparticle on the physiochemical, structural and mechanical properties of the nanocomposite films.

TC and Mung bean seeds were supplied from local markets in Urmia city, Iran and the apple pulp procured from the process wastage of manufacturing fruit chips in Sib Azar Factory in Urmia, Iran. The CeO2 nanoparticle, with 90% purity and particle size of 20 nm, was provided from US-Nano (United States) and chitosan, with moderate molecular weight and deacetylation degree of higher than 70% was provided from Sigma (Germany). Also, strains of E. Coli (ATCC 25922) and Staphylococcus aureus (ATCC 25923) (Iranian collection of industrial microorganisms), DPPH and glycerol were purchased from Merck (Germany).

To extract mung bean proteins, the alkaline and acid precipitation methods were used. To do so, Mung bean flour (ground mung sifted with mesh 60 sieves) were mixed with 5% weight/volume ratio and the pH of the mixture was set to 9 using 1 N sodium hydroxide. Then the mixture was stirred in 25 °C for 1 h and centrifuged in 4000 rpm for 10 mins. Next, the surface part of the vials was collected and pH of the mixture was set to 4.5 using 1 N HCl to precipitate the protein and was centrifuged again in 4000 rpm for 10 mins. Finally, the precipitated materials were collected and rinsed with distilled water and lyophilized and were kept at 20 °C until the analysis (Du et al., 2018).

To extract AP, 10 g of apple pulp ground in warm bath was added into a 0.1 N HCl with 25:1 v/w ratio and centrifuged at 1500 rpm for 60 min after reaching room temperature. Then, the pH level at the upper part of the tube was set to 2 using 0.1 N NaOH. Then the pectin was precipitated using 2 times ethanol (96%). Next, the sample was relaxed for a 12 h interval until the generation of two phases. The precipitated part was then rinsed two times with 60% ethanol, followed by another two times rising using 70% ethanol and one final rinsing using 96% ethanol. Next, the derived pectin was pressed to remove the alcohol. The sample was then placed in a 40 °C vacuum oven and dried until reaching a consistent weight. The obtained pectin was subsequently kept in 20 °C in darkness (Adetunji et al., 2017).

To prepare graphite carbon quantum dots, two graphite electrodes, with the diameter of 0.5 cm, as anodes and cathodes and a 0.1 M ethanol/NaOH solution as the electrolyte were used. 1 cm of each electrode were submerged in the electrolyte and a 100-mA current was applied. Subsequently, the electrochemical exfoliation was initiated in the anode and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were generated in different sizes. Next, the solution changed color from colorless to bright yellow after 3 h and then to a very pale red. Next, the solution was placed in an ultrasonic bath for 30 mins and was filtered through a whatman filter paper. The obtained transparent solution was then kept in the refrigerator at 4 °C for use (Borna et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2015).

The extract extraction process of dry and powdered TC seeds was carried out using percolation method. In this method, 250 g TC powder was poured into a percolator along with 96% ethanol. After 72 h, the extraction process was performed. Next, 96% ethanol was added to the remaining powder and the extraction process was repeated after 8 h. The process was further repeated after 24 h for the third time. The obtained extracts of the first to third stage were collected in a glass container and were concentrated using a distillation device in vacuum (Gomaa et al., 2019).

To encapsulate TC extract, chitosan, as the membrane material, was dissolved in 50 mL acetic acid and TCE, with the extract to biopolymer ratio of 40:60, was added to the solution. To improve the dissolving process of the emulsion, 0.003 g of tween 80 as well as high rate ultra-turrax were used. Finally, the solution was prepared by placing it in an ultrasonic (20 KHz) 150 W power bath for 5 mins (Sani & Marand, 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of particle size, particle size index and zeta potential of TCE microcapsule
ce:section.ce:para: To determined the particle size (Z-average), particle size index (PDI) and surface electrical charge against pH, known as zeta potential, a Nano ZS (Maroon, England) zeta sizer device was used. The test result was obtained at 25 °C. Prior to the test, the dispersion was diluted with de-ionized water in 1:100 ratio (Karimi Sani et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of IMBP -AP nanocomposite film containing TCEM and CeO2 nanoparticles and CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare the nanocomposite of the film, 1.25 g IMBP was added to 50 mL of distilled water and the pH of the derived solution was set to 8 using 0.1 N NaOH. The solution was then stirred using a magnetic stirrer at 80 °C for 30 mins. Next, 1.25 g apple pectin was mixed with 50 mL deionized water at 30 °C for 12 h using a magnetic stirrer which led to the two polymers generating a composite. Following that, TCEM was added to polymer solutions at different levels proportionate to the dry matter of the polymer. Next, the solution was homogenized using a homogenizer (D9I12, HEIDOLPH, Germany) at 13,000 rpm for 2 mins. Additionally, based on the statistical design, the CeO2 nanoparticles was also added to the solution at different levels proportionate to the dry matter of the polymer and were homogenized on the stirrer for 10 mins. Next, CQDs were added at different levels to the composite solution of the film. Then, glycerol (40% the weight of the total dry matter) was added as a softener. Then, the solution was stirred for 15 mins and further was aired for 10 mins. Finally, 25 mL of the obtained solution as poured into the center of a plate which was moved into a 38 °C oven for the film to be formed. The produced film was evaluated after drying (Silva et al., 2018 ؛ Seydim & Sarikus, 2006; Moghadam et al., 2020).

To determine the new bonds in the film sample structure, the samples were first prepared as thin tablets (< 1 mm) using potassium bromide method. Then, they were examined by an FTIR spectrophotometer (Spectrum Two, Perkin Elemer, USA) in 4000–400 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1 (Karimi et al., 2019).

The crystalline structure of the films was investigated in an X-ray device (Bruker AXS, Karlsruhe, Germany) in 40 kV, 30 mA, 0.154 nm wavelength settings. The sample scanning was carried out from 5° to 80° with 0.5-degree intervals (Karimi Sani et al., 2019).

To determine the DSC of the films, 0.03 g of the samples were placed in a sealed aluminum pan and analysed via the DSC device (PT Linsies-10, Germany) at 10 °C/min, 0–500 °C degree range, 45 mins settings in presence of helium gas (Sani, Geshlaghi et al., 2021).

To examine the morphologic features, the samples were attached via carbon tapes and covered with gold particles in 45 s. The sample imaging was carried out from three different spots at 500 times magnification via SEM (Philip, Netherland) (Pirsa et al., 2018).

To determine the fluorescence of the prepared film, a fluorometer was used. 100 microliters of the film extract was diluted in 2 mL of distilled water. The diluted solution was then transferred into a quartz cell for measurement and the fluorescence was tested with a simulation wavelength of 280 nm and dispersion wavelengths ranging from 300 to 500 nm with the dispersion gap size set at 10 nm (Zhao et al., 2020).

The film samples were cut in 80 × 10 mm and were conditioned at 55% relative moisture for 48 h. Then, tensile strength, Young’s Modulus, and elongation at breakpoint of the films were measured using a texture analyser (TA.TX. plus, texture analyser) with 50 mm initial gap between the two jaws of the device and at a speed of 1 mm/sec (Pirsa et al., 2020). The mechanical parameters were calculated using the following equations:
(1) Tensile strength (TS) = FMAX / A
(2) Strain to break (STB) =( LMAX/ L)*100

In which, A is transverse cross section of the film (m2), Fmax is the maximum force at break point (N), F is the force (N), Lmax is elongation at the break (m), L0 is the initial length of the film(m).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Studying the release of extract from the film
ce:section.ce:para: The releasing process of the film samples was evaluated with 95% ethanol, as a food material simulant. In brief, the film samples were cut into 2 × 2 cm2 and immersed in the vials containing 10 mL of the simulant. Then, the vials were sealed and were kept in different temperature conditions (4 and 25 °C) for 72 h with two times shacking per day. Finally, the amount of the released extract was measured using UV-Vis spectrophotometer in 329 nm of wavelength (Lian et al., 2020).

TEM is an electronic microscope in which an electron ray passes through an ultra-thin sample and generates an image as a result of the interaction between the passing electrons and the sample. TEM can provide considerable high-resolution images compared to optical microscopes due to the shorter wavelength of the electrons compared to the wavelength of the visible light. In this study a TEM device with an acceletor voltage of 300 Kv was used to evaluate the particle size, morphology, shape and the distribution of CQDs.

The thickness of the films was measured using a digital micrometer (Mitutoyo-Co, Japan) with 0.001 mm precision in 10 random and different positions. The average of the mentioned measurements was used in the calculations (Sani & Marand, 2021).

To measure the moisture level of the films, the film samples were first weighted and were placed in an oven at 110 °C for 3 h until reaching a stable weight. Finally, the moisture level of the films was determined using the following equation (Nisar et al., 2018).

In which, M1 and M2 are initial weight and final weight after the samples dried, respectively.

To determin the solubility, the films were cut in 2 × 2 cm2 pieces and were kept in an oven at 105 °C for 24 h. Then, the samples were placed in a flask containing 50 mL distilled water and were moved to a shaker incubator and retained for further 24 h at 25 °C. Finally, the films were placed in an oven for 24 h at 105 °C and subsequently weighted (Nisar et al., 2018). The film solubility was then calculated using the following equation:
(4)

In which Wi and Wf are the initial and final weights of the film samples, respectively.

The WVP of the films was carried out according to Shen et al. (2021) method. To conduct this test, the cut films were placed on the lids of vials containg 3 g calcium sulfate dihydrate. The vials were placed in a desiccator containing potassium sulfate after weighing in order to increase its relative moisture to 97% at 25 °C. The vials were then weighed at definite intervals for 72 h. The amount film-transferred moisture that was abosorbed by calcium sulfate was determined from the weight added to the vials. Also, the water vapor transfer rate was determined using the slope obtained from the regression analysis of the film-transferred moisture at a definite duration. Then, water vapor transfer rate (WVTR) of the films was used to determined the level of water vapor permeation (Shen et al., 2021).
(5)

(6)

WVTR × X

In which, WVTR is the rate of water vapor transfer (g/m2h), X is the film thickness (m), A is the surface area of the film (m2) WVP is water vapor permeation (g.mm/m2. Pa.s), ΔP is the difference in the water vapor pressure between the inner and outer surface of the film in the vials (ΔP=3169 Pa).

To determine the antioxidant capacity, 0.025 g of the film samples were mixed with 4 mL methanol of which 1 mL was mixed with 1 mL of 1 mMol DPPH and was kept in the dark for 1 h. The absorption rate of the samples were then measured by a spectrophotometer (Model T60 UV, USA) at 517 nm wavelength (Sani et al., 2022). The antioxidant activity of the samples was determined using the following equation.

Photocatalytic characteristic of the IMBP -AP-based film was evaluated using metylen blue (MB) method in aqueous solution at the room temperature (Lin et al., 2018). In brief, 50 mg of the film samples were suspended into a beaker containing 50 mL of methylene blue (15 mg/L) for coloration on the film surface. Next, the suspension was horizontally beamed with a UV lamp under constant orbital stirring. After 60 mins of beaming, 5 mL of the colorized solution was taken and the concentration of MB in the solution was determined using UV-Vis spectroscopy at 664 nm wavelength. The tests were carried out in triplicates for each sample. The degradation efficiency (%) of MB was then calculated using the following equation.
(8)
Degradation efficiency % =
Ci − Ct
Ci
× 100
In which Ci is the initial concentration of MB (15 mg/L) and Ct is the concentration of the color at the t time (mg/L).

Agar diffusion test was used to examine the antimicrobial activity of the films. In this method, the films were cut into 15 mm discs and were placed in Mueller–Hinton agar plate containing S. Aureus and E. Coli O157: H7 (ATCC 25922). Prior to use, the bacteria were re-cultured two times in Mueller–Hinton agar and the plates were inoculated with 0.1 mL of broth containing 105 – 106 CFU/mLbacteria and were incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. Next, the transparent growth inhibition zone was measured using an accurate caliper (Asdagh et al., 2021).

In this study, three independent variables including TCEM, CeO2 and CQD were investigated in three levels and 16 treatments ( Table 1) using response surface methodology (RSM) of Box-Behnken design in Design Expert 11 statistical software. To examine the goodness of fit of the models, the analysis of variance and regression analyses were used and the type I error level was set to 0.05. Also, desirability function was applied for optimization (Table 4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: CQD tests (FT-IR, XRD, SEM, and TGA)

ce:section.ce:para: The XRD pattern of the CQD is displayed in Fig. (1-a). It can be observed that there is a singular peak at θ2–21° which was due to presence of carbon atoms in the carbon plane indicating a hexagonal mesh in the graphite structure (El-Shamy, 2019a). Similar finding was found in the study of Zhang et al. (2013). Additionally, the width and the extent of the peak in XRD of the CQD can be attributed to the small size of the CQD nanoparticles (El-Shamy, 2019a).

FT-IR method was used to investigate the effects of synthesis method and the applied current on the chemical structure of the synthesized nanoparticle. The FT-IR spectrum of CQD is shown in Fig. (1 -b). The FT-IR absorbtion pattern of the carbon sample can be observed at 400–4000 cm−1 range. It was found that the observed peak at 3193 cm−1 was associated with O-H group (Riahi et al., 2022) and the peaks present at 1644 and 1729 cm−1 was associated with CO group (Zhang et al., 2013). Additionally, the observed peak at 1713 cm−1 was associated with CO group. Also, the distinguished peak at 1409 cm−1 was associated with either C-O or C-N amine groups (Ţucureanu et al., 2016). The observed at 1193 was due to C-O cm−1 bond (Riahi et al., 2022). The observable peak at 643 cm−1 was associated to C-H bond (Zhang et al., 2013).

Fig. (1 -c) shows the TGA anaylsis of CQD. According to the results, the CQDs were initially stable but after reaching 120–180 °C temperature, weight reduction occurred which can be explained by the water molecules linked with CQDs. Another weight reduction was also observed at 230–270 °C temperature range which might be due to the species that have strong covalent bonds with CQD (Kumar et al., 2014). The maximum observed disintegration temperature for the CQD, which can be determined from the DTG thermal curve peak, was 171 °C. Kumar et al. (2014) reported a primary and a secondary weight reduction at 188 and 444 °C for CDQ, respectively.

The fluorescence intensity of the CQD is one of the important defining factors of these particles and is vital to material identification and analysis. The type of manufacturing and the utilized raw materials in CQD synthesis is impactful in the fluorescence intensity of CQDs. The intensity of the applied current for synthesis of CQDs is highly important in the electrochemical method. In fact, the more intense the applied current, the weaker the fluorescence intensity of the synthesized CQDs will be. This can be described by the fact that increased intensity, increases the required potential energy for generation of CQDs and the formed CQDs will be attached to each other, leading to reduced surface-to-volume ratio which consequently decreases the fluorescence intensity. In other words, in low current intensities, the particles are generated slowly and no accumulation occurs which leads to higher surface ratio and increased fluorescence. As can be seen in Fig. 1 -d, the CQD emission wavelength and the simulation wavelength were 502 nm and 388 nm, respectively.

According to the TEM images, the applied current and synthesis method are determining factors in the particle size and morphology. Fig. 1 -e shows that the CQD particle size appears as large and bulky. By applying increased current intensity during the production, the particle size increases which can be due to the accumulation of CQD particles as a result of high applied energy. The results of TEM imaging also supports the fluorescence test results. Therefore, it can be stated that increasing the applied current in the electrochemical method increases the particle size and decreases the fluorescence intentsity (Borna et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Particle size, PDI and zeta potential of the encapsulated TCE
ce:section.ce:para: The diameter size and distribution are important paramteres that determine the stability, the release of loaded compounds and film properties. Fig. 2 displays the zeta potential and the size of encapsulated TCE. It was observed that the particle diameter of the encapsulated extract sample was 1365 nm. The PDI shows the particle distribution index. In this criterion, the lower values indicate a narrow size distribution (Otoni et al., 2016). The PDI in the range of 0.1–0.25 is an indicator of particle stability and uniformity, suggesting that the encapsulated TCE with low PDI (0.203) was sufficiently stable and uniform to be used in the film manufacturing solutions (Zhang et al., 2021). Another important factor is the zeta potential which determines the pure surface load and the degree of stability in a colloid system. In case of a zeta potential higher than 30 mV, the electrostatic repulsion can be strong enough to preserve the physical stability of the particle. Based on the results, the zeta potential of the encapsulated TCE was 1.67 mV, a value lower than 30 mV. This can be attributed to the non-ionic surfactant (Tween 80) utilized in this study which led to a low observed zeta potential for the encapsulation. The main effect of it is the prevention of integration and ester stability instead of an electrostatic one (Zhang et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Instrumental tests of the nanocomposite film

ce:section.ce:para: FT-IR spectroscopy of the film samples are shown in Fig. (3 -A). All the film samples demonstrated a similar absorption pattern in the 450–4000 cm−1 range. As can be seen in the blank film sample spectrum (a). The notable observed peak at 3280 cm−1 was due to the overlapped tensile vibrations of O-H and N-H groups present in the pectin and MPI structure (Moghadam et al., 2020). The observed peak at 1740 cm−1 was due to the presence of CO group linked to the ester bonds of the pectin. Also, the observed notable peak in 1621 cm−1 was attributed to the presence of amide I (primarily, attributed to the tensile vibrations of CO) and II (attributed to the flexural and tensile vibrations of N-H and C-N groups, respectively) of MPI (Moghadam et al., 2020) and the symmetrical and asymmetrical vibrations of carboxyle groups of pectin (Roy & Rhim, 2021).

The observed peak at 1408 cm−1 is an indicative of C-C vibration bonds in the aromatic ring. The observed peaks at 1022 and 1230 cm−1 demonstrate the vibrational bonds of C-O carboxylic acid and ester groups (Pirsa, 2020). The peak at 919 cm−1 was suggested to be the result of N-H bonds and tensile bond of C-N (Ghadetaj et al., 2018). The peak at 852 cm−1 was associated with CC bonds of alkans (Moghadam et al., 2021). In the spectrum of encapsulated extract (b) with a chitosan membrane, the appeared peak at 2930 frequency was associated to C-H flexural bond (central carbon-hydrogen bond) in the film containing chitosan-membrane encapsulated extract which can be a reason for the relative high strength of this film. Also, the observed peak at 1150 cm−1 frequency was due to the symmetrical and asymmetrical tensile vibrations of C-O bond illustrating the saccharide-based structure of the chitosan (Sani et al., 2019).

It was found that the different observed peaks demonstrate the electrostatic interactions between pectin, Mung bean protein and the capsulated extract. Additionally, different peaks were observed at the spectrum of the nanoparticles (c) suggesting an electrostatic interaction between pectin, MPI and CeO2. In the CQD spectrum (d), no novel peak was observed in the FT-IR of the film samples. In the optimized film spectrum (e), all the peaks related to CeO2, microencapsulated extract and CQD substantiates the presence of the particles in the composite film and the relocation of the peaks confirms the electrostatic interactions between the components of the composite as well. Similar findings were reported by Roy and Rhim (2021) by the addition of melanine nanoparticles and the grapefruit seed extract to a pectin -agar based film.

The resuls of XRD of the film samples are shown in Fig. (3 -B). Based on the XRD of the blank film (a), a notable peak can be found at 2θ = 22° which signifies the crystalline structure of the pectin and structural characteristics of MPI in the blank film (Akalin et al., 2021; Xiao et al., 2020) as the addition of the encapsulated TCE (b) to the IMBP -AP-based film generated no novel peak at the XRD pattern. This indicates the absence of the chemical interaction between the extract and the film matrix. This addition has also reduced the crystalline structure of the film compared to the blank which might be due to non-crystalline nature of the extract compounds that can allow them to permeate into the IMBP -AP. Additionally, the membrane of the extracted microcapsule, which is made of chitosan, can enter the crystalline structure of the film, thereby reducing the crystalline structure of the film.

A consistent result was found in the study of Ghadetaj et al. (2018) with the addition of extract Grammosciadium ptrocarpum to soybean protein isolate. With the addition of CeO2 nanoparticles to the nanocomposites of the film (c), several peaks at 2θ, 56, 47, 33, 28 and 70 were generated that signify the generation of a crystalline structure following the addition of the nanoparticles. Consistent finding was also reported in the work of Shahvalizadeh et al. (2021) by the addition of zinc oxide nanoparticles to gelatin-Tragacanth based film. In addition, in the XRD pattern followed by the addition of CQD (d) to the blank sample, two peaks at 2θ = 22° and 2θ = 40° were observed in which the peak at 22° demonstrated the crystalline structure of the pectin and the peak at 40° was caused by amorphic state of the CQD ( Fig. 4-6,d). In the optimized film sample (e), it was observed that the peaks were representative of crystalline structure generation as a result of adding nanoparticles to the film samples as well. The crystalline structure of the pectin was also observed in 2θ = 22°.

The SEM test is used to investigate the microstructure and morphology of the films. The SEM images of the film samples are shown in Fig. (3 -C). As can be seen, the blank film sample (a) has a rough and rugged surface with protrusions and minute gaps. This might be due to the arrangement of the mung protein molecules during the formation of the film which is migh be done via different methods. Thus, the microstructure of the films is controlled by the molecular organization in the film mesh and is dependent on a variety of proteins, interaction of proteins with other materials in the film matrix and to the proportions of the protein components with other materials used to manufacture the films (Hoque et al., 2011).

With the addition of the encapsulated TCE to the films, a leveled and uniform surface with no cracks and gaps was produced (Fig. 3-C-c) which was due to the distribution of the nanoparticles in the film surface. The Addition of CQD to the film samples caused a large gap on the film surface (Fig. 3-C-d). Additionally, the SEM images of the optimized film sample showed holes and gaps on the surface of the film which was due to the incorporation of cerium oxide nanoparticles and CQD into the film samples (3-C-e). The obtained results were in line with the findings of Pirsa (2020) with the addition of the pectin -based clay nanoparticles abd with results of study with the addition of lemongrass and ZnO into a film based on sodium caseinate. Also, Arfat et al., (2014) found consistent results by using a film based on gelatin that contained ZnO and basil leave essentials.

The results o DSC curves of the films based on petkin and MPI and their components are shown in Fig. (3-D). The DSC curve in the blank displayed two endothermic and one exothermic peak. In this spectrum, the observed peak in the 110 was related to the glass transition temperature of pectin and protein. The observed peak at 158 indicates the melting temperature of the film and the peak in the 215 can be attributed to the thermal disintegration temperature of the film sample (3-D-a). The observed spectrum of the film sample containing encapsulated TCE was similar to the spectrum of the blank film and no changes were observed (3-D-b). With the addition of CeO2 nanoparticles the endothermic peak at 110 (glass transition temperature) became to an exothermic peak which can be due to water evaporation of film samples. Also, it led to the increase of melting and thermal disintegration temperatures of the film samples (3-D-c). In other words, the presence of nanoparticles in the pectin and protein structure has increased the thermal stability of the film. Due to high disintegration temperature of the nanoparticles and appropriate interaction between pectin, protein and the nanoparticles, the thermal stability of the film can be highly increased with the presence of the nanoparticles (Pirsa, 2020).

According to the DSC curve (3-D-d), with the addition of CQD to the film samples, the glass transition temperature increased. This can be due to the density increase of cross-sectional bonds in the polymeric matrix following the generation of internal and external hydrogenic bonds between the polymer and CQD. Additionally, the addition of CQD increased the melting and thermal disintegration temperatures of the films indicating high thermal stability of the nanocomposites. This behavior can be explained by the change in the microstructure, crystal rotation and the crystalline nature of the nanocomposite which alters the morphology and crystalline structure of the polymer. In addition, increase of thermal disintegration temperature can be due to the molecular interactions of polymer and CQD (El-Shamy, 2019a). In the optimal film samples, its bas found that the glass transition, melting, and thermal disintegration temperatures were increased compared which was attributed to the presence of CeO2 nanoparticle and CQD (3-D-e). Similar findings were reported with incorporation of clay nanoparticles to a pectin -based film (Pirsa, 2020) and addition of CQD to a polyvinyl alcohol-based film (El-Shamy, 2019a).

Fig. 3-E displays the results of fluourescene anaylsis. In the blank film (a), the film containing TCE microcapsule (b) and the film containing CeO2 (c), the emission wavelength was proximate to each other and partial changes were observed. However, in the film containing CQD (d), the observed changes were in lower fluourescene intensity compared to the optimized film suggesting that during the interaction between biopolymers, the low current intensities of the CQDs were generated gradually, leading to no accumulation, increased surface ratio of the formed particles and increased fluourescene intensity. In the optimized film sample (e), it was found that the interaction of the variables led to a shift to high fluorescene intensity. A similar result was reported by Protte et al. (2016). In addition, Fig. 3-E-f shows that the extracts of the nanocomposite films exposed to the visibile light displayed color changes. The film containg CQD and the optimized film generated a green hue after undergoing UV beaming which supports the fluorescence trait of these samples.

The results of the microbial test of the film samples are presented in Table 3 and Fig. 4-A. Accordingly, with the addition of encapsulated extract, CeO2 and CQD, the antimicrobial activity of the samples against gram positive and negative bacteria was increased which was statistically significant (p < 0.05). The maximum antimicrobial activity was observed in the optimal film samples as these samples demonstrated higher antimicrobial activity against S. Aureus (gram positive) and E. Coli (gram negative). In gram positive bacteria, the cell wall is comprised of molecules such as peptides, glycans and small amount of proteins. The cell wall of the gramm negative bacteria has more complex structure and is composed of polysaccharides, proteins and peptidoglycans. The gram-negative bacteria also ornated with an additional hydrophilic membrane with specific lipo-polysaccharide molecules that act as a barrier against hydrophilic compounds which reduces the permeation of hydrophilic compounds through the cell membrane as a result. The permeation of antimicrobial agents into the gram-positive bacteria is facilitated due to their lack of the additional outer membrane (Roy and Rhim, 2019).

Moreover, the antimicrobial effect of TCE and CeO2 is influenced by the structure, shape, the size of the nanoparticles and the presence of lypohpilic and hydrophobic functional groups in the phenolic compounds of the extract which induce the antimicrobial characteristics that, through different mechanisms, can bond to the bacteria membrane and damage the membrane and the cell wall, proteins and DNA and lead to cell death (Gohargani et al., 2020). Whatsmore, due to the minute size and high surface to volume ratio of the CQD, these particles can affect the bacteria via different means such as generation of free radicals, oxidation, interrupting cell membrane by coating the cell surface and increase the antimicrobial characteristics (Kousheh et al., 2020). Ghadetaj et al. (2018) found comparable results by adding Grammosciadium ptrocarpum extract to a film based on whey protein isolate. Also, Xiao et al. (2020) found consistent results with the addition of ZnO to a film based on soy protein isolate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extract release from the film samples
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. (4-B) displays the release of the extract from the film samples containing encapsulated TCE in 4 °C and 25 °C. As can be seen, the release of the extract from the film into the environment occurs gradually and in a regulated manner. Based on the results, the extract release rate from the optimized film sample was higher than the film containing encapsulated extract. This behavior can be due to the effect of CeO2 nanoparticles on the permeation of the capsule wall of the extract enabling facilitated release of the extract into the environment (Karimi Sani et al., 2021).

Additionally, the release rate of the extract at 4 °C was lower than the environment temperature. In other words, with the temperature increase, the mobility of the pectin polymer chains and MPI increases as well. This greatly enables the passage of extract droplets through the film matrix (Ghadetaj et al., 2018). Consistent findings were reported by Asadpour et al. (2017) by investigating the release of encapsulated folic acide in a film based on pectin and whey protein isolate and in Ghadetaj et al. (2018) study of Grammosciadium ptrocarpu extract release from a film based on whey protein isolate. Amjadi et al., (2021) have also reported parallel results for the release of cinnamon extract release from a film based in gelating containing different nanoparticles.

The mechanical strength and elongation at break of the procured film samples are presented in Table 3. According to the Table 3, the mechanical elongation at break of the film samples were increased significantly with the addition of encapsulated TCE and CeO2 nanoparticles (p < 0.05) that led to improve the mechanical properties of the film samples. This behavior can be described by the possible interaction and generation of electrostatic bonds between carboxylic or sulfhydryl groups with negative charge of specific amino acides of MPI as well as with the positively charged CeO2 complex during the film solution preparation (Malathi et al., 2017).

Also, the TCE is a phenolic compound that contains many hydroxyl groups in its structure that can be involved in the hydrophobic interactions and generation of hydrogenic bonds with amino acides which as a result can increase the film matrix resistance. The increased elongation at break of the films might be due to the interactions between the extract and MPI with the hydrogenic and covalent bonds. In addition, the polyphenols present in the extract can ac as a plasticizer and can increase the cohesion and thereby can increase the fracture strength (Malathi et al., 2017). Matching results were found by Malathi et al. (2017) regarding this finding wit the addition of TiO2 nanoparticles to a film based on soy protein isolate. Moreover, the addition of CQD to the film samples led to the increased mechanical strength and elongation at break of the samples. This can be explained by the possibility of generation of hydrogenic, Van der Waals and other chemical bonds between active groups of CQD and polymeric matrix of the film (Xu et al., 2020).

Furthermore, the increased mechanical strength can be due to the increased intra- and inter-molecular bonds in the composite films and the increase of strong interactions between the CQD and polymer matrix (El-Shamy, 2019a). The more increase in tensile strength of Max CeO2 film than TCE film may be related to the rigidity and strength of the CeO2 nanoparticles structure and high compatibility between CeO2 and the network of polymer due to the high surface area and the interactions between nanoparticles and polymer network. Consistent findings were cited by addition of CQD to a film based on polyvynil alcohol (Xu et al., 2020). According to the obtained results, the mechanical strength and elongation at break of the optimized film samples were increased, which was also statistically significant (p < 0.05). This increment in the optimized samples was due to the synergistic effect of encapsulated TCE and CeO2 nanoparticles.

MB is a cationic color that has wide applications in medicine, paper and textile industries (Pai et al., 2019). The photocatalytic degradation function of MB with CeO2 occurs when CeO2 is irradiated and the electron pairs generate cavities. After that, the superoxide radicals generate additional cavities as a result of the reactions between electrons with oxygen and free hydroxyl radicals, which creates hydroxide ions/water, that ruptures the organic bonds (Ali et al., 2018; Rafaie et al., 2017; Raja et al., 2018). The results in the Table 3 shows that in the film that contains a microcapsule, the photocatalytic feature is approximately 37%. The antioxidant compounds present in the TCE microcapsule such as rosmarinic acid, caffeic acid and ferulic acid might generate hydroxyl radicals under UV radiation that can degrade MB molecules (Noda et al., 2002).

Further, the film containing CQD displayed a photocatalytic activity of 45% as graphite absorbs visible light and displays photocatalytic protperties (Hou, Yang et al., 2013). This behavior can also enhance the transfer of surface electrons and separation of the generated electron hole pairs for the CeO2 nanoparticle (Wang et al., 2012). The obtained results of MB degradation for the IMBP -AP nanocomposite film is shown in the Table 3 (Table 3). As can be seen, the blank film displayed no photocatalytic activity. The MB degradation effect was statistically significant for all of the composite film samples. The results showed that the highest photocatalytic activity was observed in the optimized film sample which might be explained by the presence of CeO2 nanoparticle that generated interactions in the interstitials of the biopolymer and led to increased lifetime of electron hole pair (Lin et al., 2014). Also, after the optimized film, the presence of CeO2 displayed the highest photocatalytic feature which might be due to the agglomeration of the CeO2 in the biopolymer matrix and the reduction of surface ratio (Lei et al., 2012).

With regard to the ANOVA results ( Table 2), the single effects of TCE microcapsule and CeO2 on the moisture of the films containing 2.5% CQD was statistically significant (p < 0.05) since by increasing these two factors the moisture level of the samples increased as well ( Fig. 5 -a). This increase during the addition of TCE was due to reduced cross-sectional bonds among the biopolymer chains, increase of intra-molecular hydrogenic bonds among phenolic compounds in the extract, hydroxyl groups, MPI and pectin amine groups along with water molecules (Yong et al., 2019). Whatsmore, the increased moisture following the addition of CeO2 nanoparticle was attributed to the increased accessibility to OH groups for water bonding in presence of the nanoparticle and reduction of interactions between MPI/ pectin and the nanoparticle (Jamróz & Kopel, 2018). Comparable results were found in the studies of Eze et al., (2022) with the addition of phenolic extract of the broken rice in an edible film based on chitosan, Vidal et al. (2022) with the addition of green coffee essential oil in an intelligent film, Mousazadeh et al., (2021) with the addition of Cu2O to a film based on modified gelatin and Ojagh et al., (2018) with the addition of TiO2 nanoparticle in an edible film based on gelatin/agar.

According to the results of the statistical analysis (Table 2), the main effects of the TCE microcapsule, CQD and interaction of CQD/ CeO2 on the film solubility was statistically significant (p < 0.05). By increasing the amount of CQD the solubility of the samples increased and the highest solubility was observed in the samples containing the highest levels of added CQDs and CeO2 nanoparticles while the in the lowest levels of the nanoparticles, reduced solubility was observed (Fig. 5 -b). The CQD compounds display good solubility as they contain surface functional groups and high levels of hydroxyls. Therefore, by increasing their percentage in the formulation, the film solubility can be increased (Xu et al., 2020).

In low percentages, the CeO2 nanoparticles increased the interaction between the nanoparticle and the polymer chains and lead to increased hydrophobicity and finally reduced solubility of the film via hydrogenic bonds (Esfahani et al., 2020). In high percentages however, the reducd interactions between polymer chains and the nanoparticle and formation of ionic bonds between the nanoparticles and hydrophobe groups in compounds of the formulation increases the solubility of the samples (Jamróz, Juszczak et al., 2018). Similar findings regarding this were reported in the studies of Meydanju et al., (2022) with the addition of TiO2-Ag in a biodegradable film based on lemon peels and xanthan gum, Ngo et al., (2018) with the addition of oxidated nanoparticles in edible films based on pectin /alginate. Also, by adding Ag nanoparticles to the composite startch chitosan/ glucomannan-cassava films (Nair et al., 2017), ZnO nanoparticles in composite films based on CMC and okra mucilage (Mohammadi et al., 2018), oxidated nanoaprticles in biodegradable cationic starch/ montmorillonite films (Vaezi et al., 2019) the film solubility was reduced as well.

On the other hand, presence of the extract in the film formulation augmented the reduced solubility and prevented steep increase of solubility in the samples (Song et al., 2017). This was further evidenced by the application of grape seed extract in edible films based on silver carp surimi and pomegranate peels (Munir et al., 2019), fruit extract and seed oil of Berberis crataegina in edible films based on chitosan (Kaya et al., 2018) and the Codium tomentosum extract in the alginate and chitosan edible films (Augusto et al., 2018).

With regard to ANOVA results and the regression coefficients (Table 2), the main effects of the studied factors and the interactions of TCE/ CeO2 and CQD/ CeO2 on WVP of the films were statistically significant (p < 0.05). By increasing the amount of TCE, CeO2 and CQD the WVP of the samples increased. The highest WVP was observed in samples containing the most amount of the studied factors (5-c-d). the WVP for the edible films is dependent to the hydrophobic or hydrophilic nature, presence of cavities and cracks and the interaction of the compounds in the film matrix. In this study, the increased WVP of the films can be attributed to the presence of substantial amounts of hydroxyl groups such as phenolic compounds in the TCE and the hydrophilic nature of TCE (Sogut & Seydim, 2018; Sukhtezari et al., 2017).

Furthermore, the accumulation of CeO2 nanoparticles and CQD disrupts the internal mesh of the MPI/ pectin films and formation of discrete phase between the nano compounds and the polymer matrix, as the studies suggest, lead to generation of a porous structure and subsequently, increased WVP (Liu et al., 2017; Shankar et al., 2015). Similar findings were obtained with the addition of rosemary extract in films based on cassava starch (Pineros-Hernandez et al., 2017), addition of ethanol extract of freeze-dried apple powder and watery extract of applied peels in the methyl cellulose active film (Matta, Tavera-Quiroz, & Bertola, 2019), addition of grape seed extract to a chitosan-based film (Sogut & Seydim, 2018), addition of SiO2 nanoparticles to films based on soy flour (Salarbashi et al., 2018), chitin nanoparticles and ZnO to gelatin-based films (Sahraee et al., 2017), Cu2O nanoparticles to agar-based gilms (Roy & Rhim, 2019), films based on gelatin/ carrageenan with carbon dots derived from enoki fungi (Roy, Ezati et al., 2021) and a film based on cellulose nanofiber with N-CQD (Ezati et al., 2022).

With regard to the predictor regression coefficients (Table 2), the main effect of the studied factors on the thickness of the films was statistically significant (p < 0.05). According to Fig. 5 -e, by increasing the amount of TCE and CeO2, the thickness of the films increased and the highest increase of thickness was observed in samples with the most amount of TCE/ CeO2. The thickness increase of the manufactured films following the addition of CeO2 can be explained by the higher content of solid matter, good dissipation of the nanoparticle in the polymeric chain and increase in the gap between the polymeric chains (Bahrami et al., 2019). On the other hand, presence of CQD in the film formulation has enhancing effects on the thickness of the samples due to the increase in the amount of solid materials (Qiang et al., 2020).

Meanwhile, thickness increase of the films following the addition of TCE might be explained by the increase of solid material and disruption caused by the extract droplets in the polymeric chain. This can subsequently lead to the generation of pores in the biopolymer structure that can boost the thickness by absorbing additional water (Rambabu et al., 2019; Nisar et al., 2018). Similar findings concerning increased thickness were reported in the edible films based on gelatin that contain grapefruit seed extract and TiO2 (Riahi et al., 2021), edible films based on sodium alginate and mulberry leaf extract (Morus australis) (Kuan et al., 2020), biodegradable film based on chitosan containing Chinese onion root extract (Allium tuberosum) (Riaz et al., 2020).

In addition, the results of thickness increase following the addition of the nanoparticles were in accordance with the findings of Mohammadi et al. (2018) with the addition of ZnO nanoparticles in a film based on carboxy methyl cellulose and okra mucilage, findings of Alizadeh-Sani et al., (2018) with the addition of TiO2 to a bio-nano composite film based on whey protein isolate bioprotein and the results of Shankar and Rhim (2017) with the addition of silver nanoparticles in films based on agar/lignin.

With regard to the results of ANOVA nad regression coefficient analyses (Table 2), the main effect of TCE microcapsule on the antioxidant capacity of the films comprised of 12.5% nanoparticle and 2.5% of CQD was statistically significant (p < 0.05). According to Fig. 5 -f, it was observed that by increasing the amount of TCE microcapsule, the antioxidant capacity of the samples increased and the highest level of antioxidant activity was found in samples containing the most amount of TCE (7.5%). The observed high antioxidant capacity following the addition of TCE was chacarterized by presence of rich amounts of antioxidant agents in the TCE such as phenolic compounds (rosemaric acid, caffeic acid, frolic acid, etc), tannins, terpenoids, flavonoids (kaempferol, chrysene, galangin, pinocembrin, quercetin and so on) and sterols (Moulal-hacene, Boufadi, Keddari, & Homrani, 2020). Table 3.

On the other hand, the presence of CeO2 nanoparticles and CQD in the formulation has led to the enhancement of phenolic compounds and antioxidant activity (De Carvalho & Junior, 2020; García-López et al., 2018). Comparable findings regarding the increasd antioxidant activity was achieved with the addition of rosemary extract in an edible film based on fish myofibrillar protein/ chitosan (Du et al., 2021), chitosan coatings enriched with true cardamom essential and extract (Khorshidi et al., 2021), edible films based on carrageenans that contains the Lapacho tea extract (Jancikova, Dordevic, Jamroz, Behalova, & Tremlova, 2020) and biodegradable films based on ripe banana peel and starch that contains the Eriobotrya japonica leave extracts (Silva et al., 2020). Table 4.

In this study, a nanocomposite film comprised of IMBP -AP that contains microencapsulation of TCE/ CeO2 nanoparticles and CQD was prepared. Accodring to the images of FESEM, it was observed that the blank film was featured with a rough and rugged surface with bumps and small gaps. But with the addition of capsulated TCE to the film, a smooth and uniform surface with no cracks and gaps was obtained. The FT-IR results showed the electrostatic interactions between IMBP -AP in the film containing TCEM and in the film containing CeO2 nanoparticles. It was found that by increasing the percentage of CeO2 nanoparticle and TCE microcapsule and CQD, the film parameters such as the level of moisture, thickness, solubility, WVP were remarkably increased.In the film containing CeO2 nanoparticle and the film containing TCEM, it was found that the crystalline structure of the IMBP -AP was reduced. The addition of he optimized and CeO2 nanoparticle addition films led to thermal stability of the film. Th optimized films and the films containing TCEM displayed the most antimicrobial property and the optimized film showed the most mechanical feature. The release TCEM from the film to the environment occurred gradually. The highest photocatalytic characteristic was observed in the optimized film and the film containing CeO2 nanoparticles. Also, the film containing CQD and the optimized film validate the fluorescene trait.
Iraj Karimi Sani: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Mohammad Alizadeh: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Software, Writing – review & editing.
